title: Pseudorepresentations and the Eisenstein ideal
speaker: Preston Wake
abstract: Abstract: In his ladmark 1976 paper "Modular curves and the Eisenstein ideal", Mazur studied congruences modulo p between cusp forms and an Eisenstein series of weight 2 and prime level N. We use deformation theory of pseudorepresentations to study the corresponding Hecke algebra. We will discuss how this method can be used to refine Mazur's results, quantifying the number of Eisenstein congruences. We'll also discuss some partial results in the composite-level case. This is joint work with Carl Wang-Erickson.

proof_stages.label: Mazur’s prime-level Eisenstein component
proof_stages.summary: The talk sets up Mazur’s prime-level problem by defining the weight-two Eisenstein series, the Eisenstein ideal, the localized Hecke algebra and its cuspidal quotient, and recalls Mazur’s theorem that the cuspidal quotient is Z_p[[y]]/(f) with principal Eisenstein ideal and prescribed index.

proof_stages.label: Newton-polygon and rank questions
proof_stages.summary: The speaker isolates the remaining questions—especially the Newton polygon and rank of Mazur’s polynomial f, and later composite level—and uses computations to motivate the search for arithmetic criteria governing the different possible behaviors.

proof_stages.label: Galois cohomology classes a, b, and c
proof_stages.summary: For p>3 and prime N with p dividing N−1, the rank problem is translated into Galois cohomology by defining classes a, b, and c, respectively from the degree-p cyclotomic subextension, the Kummer class of N, and a class split at p, organized as entries of a cocycle for the adjoint of the residual Eisenstein representation.

proof_stages.label: Rank theorem via cup and Massey products
proof_stages.summary: The main prime-level theorem identifies rank growth of the Eisenstein Hecke algebra with vanishing of cup products, and more generally—inductively—with vanishing of higher Massey powers; p-adic refinements of these products encode the Newton polygon.

proof_stages.label: Class-group interpretation of cup-product vanishing
proof_stages.summary: Cup-product vanishing is related to noncyclic p-torsion in certain class groups by viewing the cup product as the obstruction to filling an upper-triangular matrix representation, with Massey products appearing as the corresponding higher obstruction to gluing larger triangular representations.

proof_stages.label: Mazur–Tate zeta order and Merel criterion
proof_stages.summary: The same cup-product condition is connected to the Mazur–Tate order of vanishing of a mod-p Dirichlet L-value element in a group ring, yielding a new algebraic route to Merel’s criterion in terms of the Merel number and clarifying that higher zeta orders and Hecke ranks need not agree beyond the first cases.

proof_stages.label: Squarefree composite-level Eisenstein setup
proof_stages.summary: For squarefree composite level, the speaker explains that there are many weight-two Eisenstein series indexed by Atkin–Lehner signs, computes their constant-term congruence conditions, and fixes the sign pattern with one minus sign and the remaining signs plus, using the Hecke algebra generated by unramified Hecke operators and Atkin–Lehner involutions.

proof_stages.label: Composite-level Gorenstein theorems
proof_stages.summary: The composite-level results assert Gorenstein and local-complete-intersection structure, describe exact sequences and generators of Eisenstein ideals, relate Gorensteinness to freeness and multiplicity one, and contrast the tractable one-minus-sign case with the more delicate two-minus-sign case where p-th-power splitting conditions govern Gorenstein behavior.

examples.name: Prime level N=11 at p=5
examples.note: The unique weight-two cusp form at level 11 is congruent to the Eisenstein series mod 5, illustrating the generic linear case where the localized cuspidal Hecke algebra is just Z_5.
examples.engagement_reason: He works through the N=11, p=5 case by giving the one-dimensional cusp space/q-expansion, checking the Eisenstein congruence, and concluding the Eisenstein polynomial is linear and T0 ≅ Zp.

passes.reason: The pair N=11,p=5 is used as the baseline ordinary linear/rank-one Eisenstein congruence case, not to refute a claim.
ordinary prime-level Eisenstein example with linear f and rank-one Hecke algebra, used as the baseline behavior

N=11 is the minimal p=5 prime level giving the basic rank-one linear Eisenstein congruence, used illustratively rather than adversarially.

examples.name: Prime level N=31 at p=5
examples.note: The two-dimensional cusp space is one Galois orbit with 5 ramified, illustrating an irreducible quadratic Eisenstein factor rather than the usual linear behavior.
examples.engagement_reason: He analyzes the N=31, p=5 case via the two conjugate forms, the ramified prime above 5, and the resulting congruence to conclude the polynomial is an irreducible quadratic.

passes.reason: The pair N=31,p=5 is an ordinary prime-level case used to show the usual linear Hecke-algebra behavior is not universal, since the polynomial is irreducible quadratic.
ordinary prime-level case but used to show the usual linear behavior of f does not always occur

examples.name: Prime level N=751 at p=5
examples.note: Among two Galois orbits only one is Eisenstein at two unramified primes above 5, illustrating a reducible quadratic Hecke factor and a nontrivial Newton polygon.
examples.engagement_reason: He computes structural data for N=751, p=5—dimension, Galois orbits, primes above 5, which congruences hold—and uses it to conclude the Eisenstein polynomial is a reducible quadratic.

passes.reason: The pair N=751,p=5 is structurally ordinary but is used to show further failure of the simple linear pattern, with a reducible quadratic Eisenstein factor.

examples.name: Prime level N=3001 at p=5
examples.note: This larger computation gives Eisenstein factors of degrees 3, 2, and 1, illustrating that the rank and factorization of the Eisenstein component can become substantially more complicated.
examples.engagement_reason: He only reports the final computed outcome for N=3001, p=5—that the polynomial has factors of degrees 3, 2, and 1 and rank 6—while explicitly not working through the large numerical data.

passes.reason: The pair N=3001,p=5 is not a sporadic or extremal object, but it is used to rule out simple low-rank/few-factor expectations by giving factors of degrees 3, 2, and 1.
